did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and machine learning for skin lesion classification. Specifically, it fine-tunes pre-trained deep learning models (Densenet-201 and Inception-v3) to extract hybrid deep features from dermoscopic images. It then employs a Binary Tree Growth (BTG) optimization algorithm for feature selection and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for the final multi-class categorization of skin lesions using the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel skin lesion classification framework using deep learning (Densenet-201, Inception-v3) and a Binary Tree Growth (BTG) optimization algorithm. It details specific methodology, hyper-parameters, datasets (ISIC2018, ISIC2019), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, etc.) and comparisons with existing methods, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Blend of Deep Features and Binary Tree Growth Algorithm for Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-aided skin lesion classification framework that blends hybrid deep features extracted from fine-tuned Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 models. A Binary Tree Growth (BTG) optimization algorithm is employed for feature selection to reduce dimensionality and improve classification performance. The selected features are then fed into a multi-class Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for categorization. The framework was evaluated on the challenging ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 dermoscopic image datasets using a 20:80 test-to-train split. The proposed method achieved high performance metrics, including 98.50% accuracy on ISIC2018 and 96.60% accuracy on ISIC2019. These results demonstrate that the blend of deep features and BTG-based optimization outperforms several existing approaches in skin lesion classification.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion categories (e.g., melanoma, benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion, nevus, actinic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images. It extracts hybrid deep features from fine-tuned Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 models, selects features using a Binary Tree Growth (BTG) algorithm, and classifies them using a Support Vector Machine (SVM). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset collected from the Medical University of Vienna (MUV), Austria, and C. R. skin cancer practice in Queensland, Australia. It contains images scanned from film negatives and digitally processed.

use.training: Used for training the fine-tuned Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 models and the SVM classifier, split 80% of the data.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions a 20:80 test-to-train split.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification performance, split 20% of the data.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset comprising images from previous ISIC challenges, grouped by actual datasets to avoid duplication.

use.training: Used for training the fine-tuned Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 models and the SVM classifier, split 80% of the data.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions a 20:80 test-to-train split.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification performance, split 20% of the data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper does not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons are made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2018.primary_model: SVM with BTG-optimized hybrid features ([ F1 F2 ](o ))
ISIC2018.notes: The proposed framework with SVM classifier outperformed k-NN and Random Forest. The blend of Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 features achieved the best results among deep feature combinations. BTG-based feature selection outperformed other metaheuristic algorithms (ACO, BHHO, BDA, WOA, BPSO, BASO) in terms of overall performance and feature dimension reduction.

ISIC2019.primary_model: SVM with BTG-optimized hybrid features ([ F1 F2 ](o ))
ISIC2019.notes: The 'bcc' and 'df' classes were noted as the best and worst learned by the model respectively in terms of error rates, though 'df' had the highest AUC. The proposed feature set [ F1 F2 ](o ) showed notable improvement over other feature vectors.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid deep feature extraction using fine-tuned Densenet-201 and Inception-v3, followed by Binary Tree Growth (BTG) optimization and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier
architecture_used.pretraining: Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (1000 classes) and then fine-tuned on skin lesion data (ISIC2018 and ISIC2019).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported; images were processed via dermoscopy and resized/processed for the specific models.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hybrid deep features were extracted from both models. Features from Densenet-201 denoted as [F1], features from Inception-v3 denoted as [F2].
architecture_details.optimization_algorithm: Binary Tree Growth (BTG) algorithm was used for feature selection to reduce dimensionality and remove redundant/irrelevant features from the hybrid feature set.

training_details.augmentation: Data augmentation was applied during fine-tuning (results reported with and without augmentation).

classification_or_output_head.input_to_classifier: Optimized hybrid feature vector [F1 F2](o) with dimension 1983 (for ISIC2018) or similar optimized dimensions for ISIC2019.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation applied during fine-tuning of Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 models (results reported with and without augmentation)

Note: The paper acknowledges that hair occlusion is a limitation and suggests hair removal as future work, but does not explicitly report applying specific hair/artifact removal techniques in the current study.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Flips/turn over images with 50 percent probability in every dimension
Shearing angle varying randomly between -30 and 30

conclusions: The proposed framework, which blends hybrid deep features from fine-tuned Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 models with Binary Tree Growth (BTG) optimization and an SVM classifier, outperforms many existing approaches for skin lesion classification.
The blend of features from Densenet-201 and Inception-v3 is complementary, providing improved discriminating features compared to individual models or other deep learning combinations.

The BTG-based optimization algorithm effectively selects significant features by removing redundant or irrelevant ones, leading to superior classification performance compared to other metaheuristic feature selection methods.
The proposed method achieved high performance metrics on the ISIC2018 dataset, with 98.50% accuracy, 96.60% sensitivity, 97.84% precision, and 99.59% specificity.
The proposed method achieved strong performance on the ISIC2019 dataset, with 96.60% accuracy, 94.21% sensitivity, 96.38% precision, and 99.39% specificity.
The SVM classifier demonstrated superiority over k-NN and Random Forest classifiers for skin lesion categorization when using the optimized hybrid feature set.
Data augmentation during the fine-tuning of deep learning models significantly improved classification performance, particularly in sensitivity and F1-score.

The 'bkl' (Benign Keratosis) and 'vasc' (Vascular Lesion) classes were perfectly learned by the model on the ISIC2018 dataset, while the 'ak' (Actinic Keratosis) class had the highest error rate (12.7%).

The study demonstrates that constructing a hybrid deep-feature set and applying BTG-based optimization can effectively build symmetry between categories and reduce categorizing errors in skin lesion classification.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets which may introduce dataset-specific bias.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.
The paper acknowledges that hair occlusion in dermoscopic images is a limitation that may affect classification results, but no hair removal preprocessing was applied.
The dataset split was a single random 80:20 train-test split without cross-validation, which may lead to high variance in performance estimates.
The authors declared no external funding and no conflicts of interest, but the work was supported by an AICTE Doctoral Fellowship.

The proposed method achieved perfect learning (100% accuracy) for 'Benign Keratosis' and 'Vascular Lesion' classes on ISIC2018, which may indicate class imbalance or lack of difficulty in distinguishing these specific classes.
Actinic Keratosis ('ak') had the highest error rate (12.7%) on ISIC2018, suggesting potential difficulty in distinguishing this class from others.

The implementation details for the Binary Tree Growth (BTG) algorithm parameters are provided, but the computational complexity and scalability for larger datasets are not extensively discussed beyond runtime for feature selection.
